text: The surgeon transects the pars flaccida or gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the left gastric artery in the dissection field. Four clips are placed on the artery, which is then transected using a harmonic scalpel. The right pillar of the right cruse is identified, and the phrenoesophageal membrane is incised. The esophagus is gently separated from the right cruse, and dissection continues over the anterior esophagus to expose the diaphragmatic hiatus. The left pillar of the right cruse is subsequently identified.

The surgeon performs progressive dissection into the hiatus, mobilizing the esophagus to restore its position below the diaphragm. Blunt dissection is used to peel small arterial branches from the esophagus, resulting in minimal blood loss. The posterior vagus nerve is identified, and care is taken to avoid aggressive dissection near the aorta. A complete circumferential dissection creates a window underneath the esophagus, with both pillars of the right cruse clearly defined.

The surgeon mobilizes the fundus of the stomach by separating the greater curvature from the omentum. The gastrosplenic ligament is approached, and the short gastric arteries are divided using a harmonic scalpel. The gastrophrenic ligament is also divided, allowing complete mobilization of the fundus. The dissection plane connects with the left cruce, which is visible.

The surgeon exposes the hiatal hernia by retracting the esophagus anteriorly. The left and right pillars of the right cruce are re-approximated to repair the paraesophageal hernia. Pledgets are used to disperse the force of the sutures. The inferior vena cava is visible to the right of the right pillar, and the aorta is deep to the left pillar.

The surgeon advances an instrument beyond the paraesophageal repair to check for tightness, ensuring the esophagus is not obstructed. The nasogastric tube is withdrawn, and the fundus is directed through the window posterior to the esophagus. The shoe shine maneuver is performed to prevent twists or tension in the stomach. The fundoplication is sutured into position with permanent sutures, incorporating the muscular layer of the esophagus with the superior suture to prevent migration. The fundoplication is secured with three interrupted sutures, without pledgets, due to the thickness of the gastric wall.

The fundoplication is positioned at the eleven o'clock orientation. Metallic clips are placed on each of the three fundoplication sutures for future verification via abdominal x-ray. The liver retractor is released, marking the completion of the operation.
